title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.1 Profitability Analysis by Category

# Calculate profit metrics by category
category_profit <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit),
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Count = n()
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Profit))

category_profit

# A tibble: 3 × 5
  Category        Total_Profit Avg_Profit Total_Sales Count
  <chr>                  <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> <int>
1 Technology           146543.      78.6      839893.  1865
2 Office Supplies      126023.      20.6      731893.  6128
3 Furniture             19730.       8.96     754748.  2201



3.1.1 Visualization: Profit by Category

ggplot(superstore, aes(x = reorder(Category, Profit, FUN = sum), y = Profit, fill = Category)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  stat_summary(fun = sum, geom = "point", shape = 23, size = 4, fill = "red") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Profit Distribution by Category",
    subtitle = "Red diamonds indicate total profit per category",
    x = "Category",
    y = "Profit per Transaction",
    caption = "Source: Superstore Dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    legend.position = "none"
  )














Tip



Finding: Technology products show the highest profitability with both higher median profit and total profit ($146,543), despite having fewer transactions than Office Supplies.

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.3 The Discount Problem

# Analyze relationship between discount and profitability
discount_analysis <- superstore %>%
  mutate(
    Profit_Status = ifelse(Profit > 0, "Profitable", "Unprofitable"),
    Discount_Range = cut(Discount, 
                        breaks = c(0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0),
                        labels = c("0-10%", "11-20%", "21-30%", "31-50%", "50%+"),
                        include.lowest = TRUE)
  )

# Compare average discount
discount_comparison <- superstore %>%
  mutate(Profit_Status = ifelse(Profit > 0, "Profitable", "Unprofitable")) %>%
  group_by(Profit_Status) %>%
  summarise(
    Avg_Discount = mean(Discount),
    Count = n(),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit)
  )

discount_comparison

# A tibble: 2 × 4
  Profit_Status Avg_Discount Count Total_Profit
  <chr>                <dbl> <int>        <dbl>
1 Profitable          0.0804  8228      449336.
2 Unprofitable        0.469   1966     -157039.



3.3.1 Visualization: Discount vs Profitability

p1 <- ggplot(superstore, aes(x = Discount, y = Profit)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "#3498db") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#e74c3c", linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Discount vs Profit Relationship",
    x = "Discount Rate",
    y = "Profit"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(discount_analysis, aes(x = Discount_Range, fill = Profit_Status)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Profitable" = "#2ecc71", "Unprofitable" = "#e74c3c")) +
  labs(
    title = "Profitability Rate by Discount Range",
    x = "Discount Range",
    y = "Percentage of Transactions",
    fill = "Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p1 + p2














Important



Major Insight:

Profitable orders average 8% discount
Unprofitable orders average 48% discount
Clear negative correlation: higher discounts = lower profitability
Transactions with 50%+ discount are predominantly unprofitable

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.5 Temporal Analysis

# Prepare data for time series analysis
monthly_trends <- superstore %>%
  mutate(
    Year = year(`Order Date`),
    Month = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month")
  ) %>%
  group_by(Month, Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Calculate overall monthly metrics
monthly_overall <- superstore %>%
  mutate(Month = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month")) %>%
  group_by(Month) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Profit_Margin = (Total_Profit / Total_Sales) * 100
  )


3.5.1 Visualization: Sales and Profit Trends

p5 <- ggplot(monthly_trends, aes(x = Month, y = Total_Sales, color = Category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Sales Trend by Category",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Total Sales",
    color = "Category"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p6 <- ggplot(monthly_overall, aes(x = Month, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2c3e50", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Profit Margin Trend",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Profit Margin (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p5 / p6

text:
4.1 Summary of Critical Findings

# Create summary table of key metrics
key_metrics <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total Transactions",
    "Total Sales",
    "Total Profit",
    "Overall Profit Margin",
    "Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Loss from Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Most Profitable Category",
    "Least Profitable Sub-Category"
  ),
  Value = c(
    format(nrow(superstore), big.mark = ","),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Sales)),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit)),
    paste0(round((sum(superstore$Profit) / sum(superstore$Sales)) * 100, 2), "%"),
    paste0(format(sum(superstore$Profit < 0), big.mark = ","), 
           " (", round(mean(superstore$Profit < 0) * 100, 1), "%)"),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit[superstore$Profit < 0])),
    "Technology",
    "Tables"
  )
)

NoteBusiness Recommendations




Discount Strategy Review

Implement discount caps (max 30%) especially for Furniture
High discounts (50%+) are destroying profitability
Average profitable discount: 8% vs unprofitable: 48%

Product Line Optimization

Consider discontinuing or repricing Tables (-$17,753 loss)
Review Bookcases and Supplies categories
Expand Copiers and Phones product lines (highest profit margins)

Regional Focus

West region is the strongest performer ($110,799 profit)
Central region needs strategic intervention (lowest avg profit)
Consider targeted marketing in underperforming regions

Segment Strategy

Home Office segment has highest profit per transaction ($33.83)
Consumer segment provides volume; optimize for margin improvement
Corporate segment shows strong potential for growth

optimal_bins_fd <- ceiling(maths_range / bin_width_fd)

Current choice: 20 bins
Statistical recommendations: - Sturges’ rule: 10 bins (for n = 322) - Freedman-Diaconis rule: 12 bins (based on IQR = 27)
With 20 bins: Each bin width = 4.5 points, providing finer detail than statistically recommended for this dataset.
Visual interpretation: The histogram reveals slight left skew with more high performers centered around 69.3 points.

7.4.1 Gender Comparison with Density Curves

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_density()









# Calculate overlap coefficient
male_scores <- exam_data$MATHS[exam_data$GENDER == "Male"]
female_scores <- exam_data$MATHS[exam_data$GENDER == "Female"]

# Perform two-sample t-test
t_test_result <- t.test(male_scores, female_scores)
p_value <- t_test_result$p.value







ImportantStatistical Gender Comparison



Visual observation: The density curves show substantial overlap between male and female distributions.
Statistical test (Two-sample t-test): - Mean difference: 1.4 points - t-statistic: -0.623 - p-value: 0.5335
Conclusion: There is NO statistically significant difference (p = 0.5335 > 0.05) between genders. The 1.4-point difference can be attributed to random variation.
Practical significance: Even if statistically significant, a difference of 1.4 points represents only 2% of the mean score, indicating minimal practical impact.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
7.5 geom_boxplot: Five-Number Summary

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()









# Calculate quartiles and outliers
quartiles_male <- quantile(male_scores, c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75))
quartiles_female <- quantile(female_scores, c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75))

iqr_male <- IQR(male_scores)
iqr_female <- IQR(female_scores)

# Detect outliers
outliers_male <- male_scores[male_scores < (quartiles_male[1] - 1.5*iqr_male) | 
                              male_scores > (quartiles_male[3] + 1.5*iqr_male)]
outliers_female <- female_scores[female_scores < (quartiles_female[1] - 1.5*iqr_female) | 
                                  female_scores > (quartiles_female[3] + 1.5*iqr_female)]







TipBox Plot Statistical Breakdown



Male students (n = 152): - Q1 (25th percentile): 58 - Median (50th percentile): 75 - Q3 (75th percentile): 85 - IQR (spread): 27 points - Outliers: 3 student(s)
Female students (n = 170): - Q1: 60 - Median: 74 - Q3: 85 - IQR: 25 points - Outliers: 4 student(s)
Comparative insights: - Median difference: 1 points - Spread comparison: Males show 8% more variability - Middle 50%: Males range 27 points vs Females 25 points
Outlier analysis: 7 outlier(s) identified, representing 2.2% of students

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)














NoteNotched Box Plot Interpretation



Purpose of notches: Provide visual assessment of whether medians differ significantly.
Rule: If notches do not overlap, there is strong evidence (95% confidence) that medians differ.
Observation: The notches DO overlap, suggesting medians are not statistically different.
Alignment with t-test: This visual test CONFIRMS our earlier t-test conclusion (p = 0.5335).

mean_diff_from_median_female <- female_mean - quartiles_female[2]

text:
8.3 Adding Regression Lines

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=0.5)









# Calculate smoothing window
n_effective <- n_students * 0.75  # LOESS default span







NoteLOESS Smoothing Analysis



Method: Local polynomial regression (LOESS - LOcally Estimated Scatterplot Smoothing)
How it works: Fits polynomial curves to localized subsets of data
Confidence band (gray area): 95% confidence interval for the smooth curve
Observation: The smooth curve is nearly linear, suggesting a linear relationship is appropriate
Effective sample size: LOESS uses ≈242 nearest neighbors for each point



8.3.1 Linear Regression Alternative

ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5)









# Fit linear model
lm_model <- lm(ENGLISH ~ MATHS, data = exam_data)
slope <- coef(lm_model)[2]
intercept <- coef(lm_model)[1]
r_squared <- summary(lm_model)$r.squared
residual_se <- summary(lm_model)$sigma







ImportantLinear Regression Analysis



Model: ENGLISH = 24.81 + 0.611 × MATHS
Interpretation: - Intercept: 24.81 - predicted English score when Maths = 0 (extrapolation, not meaningful) - Slope: 0.611 - for every 1-point increase in Maths, English increases by 0.611 points - R-squared: 0.691 - 69.1% of English score variance explained by Maths
Model fit quality: - Residual standard error: 8.19 points - Typical prediction is off by ±8.19 points
Practical example: - Student scoring 70 in Maths → predicted English: 67.6 ± 8.19 - Student scoring 90 in Maths → predicted English: 79.8 ± 8.19
Model adequacy: R² = 0.691 indicates moderate predictive power - Maths score is a useful predictor of English performance.

Horizontal bars (coord_flip): - Better for: Long category names, many categories, easier comparison - This case: 4 racial groups - horizontal makes category labels more readable - Comparison: Easier to compare bar lengths left-to-right than bottom-to-top

actual_y_range <- c(min(exam_data$ENGLISH), max(exam_data$ENGLISH))







WarningMisleading Aspect Ratio Issue



Problem identified: - X-axis range: 9 to 99 (range: 90) - Y-axis range: 21 to 96 (range: 75)
Why this misleads: Different axis ranges distort the visual slope of the regression line. A 1-point change in Maths appears different from a 1-point change in English.
Solution: Use equal scales (0-100 for both) to accurately represent relationship.



ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +

  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100))














TipFixed Coordinates Analysis



Improvement: Both axes now 0-100
Visual accuracy: The regression line’s angle now truly represents the relationship strength
Slope interpretation: With equal scales, slope = 0.611 means: - English gains are slightly less than Maths gains
Honest visualization: Equal scales prevent exaggerating or minimizing the correlation (r = 0.831)

TipTheme Selection Guidelines



theme_gray() (default): - Gray background with white gridlines - Best for: Screen viewing, presentations - Drawback: Less professional for publications
theme_classic(): - White background, no gridlines, only axes - Best for: Academic papers, traditional plots - Advantage: Minimal ink, maximum data focus
theme_minimal(): - White background with subtle gridlines - Best for: Modern reports, dashboards - Balance: Clean but retains helpful gridlines
For this data (n = 322, 4 racial groups): All three effectively display the distribution. Choice depends on context: - Publication: theme_classic() - Presentation: theme_gray() - Web/dashboard: theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
3.2 The Solution: Using ggrepel

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +

Using geom_label_repel() instead of geom_label() successfully eliminates label overlap through intelligent automatic positioning. Each of the 322 student IDs is now readable and traceable to its corresponding data point, while maintaining the visualization of the strong positive correlation (r = 0.831) between subjects.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
3.3 Extended Analysis: Selective Labeling Strategy

# Calculate performance metrics
exam_data_labeled <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    total_score = MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE,
    performance_label = case_when(
      total_score >= quantile(total_score, 0.9) ~ ID,
      total_score <= quantile(total_score, 0.1) ~ ID,
      TRUE ~ ""
    )
  )

# Identify thresholds
top_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.9)
bottom_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.1)
n_labeled <- sum(exam_data_labeled$performance_label != "")

ggplot(data=exam_data_labeled, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = total_score), size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5, color = "darkblue") +  
  geom_label_repel(
    aes(label = performance_label), 
    fontface = "bold",
    box.padding = 0.5,
    point.padding = 0.3,
    segment.color = 'grey50',
    max.overlaps = 20
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths: Top/Bottom 10% Performers Labeled",
    subtitle = "Color gradient shows total score across all subjects",
    x = "Mathematics Score",
    y = "English Score",
    color = "Total Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")














NoteStrategic Labeling Analysis



Selective Approach: Instead of labeling all 322 students, this visualization strategically labels only 68 students (21.1% of the sample) - specifically the top and bottom 10% performers.
Performance Thresholds: - Top 10%: Total score ≥ 254 points - Bottom 10%: Total score ≤ 118.2 points
Key Insight: The color gradient reveals that high performers in Mathematics tend to also score high in English (note the concentration of yellow/bright points in the upper-right quadrant), confirming the strong correlation (r = 0.831) and suggesting that academic ability is relatively consistent across subjects for exceptional students.

3.1 The Problem: Overlapping Labels

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), hjust = .5, vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")









# Calculate correlation for interpretation
cor_maths_english <- cor(exam_data$MATHS, exam_data$ENGLISH)







WarningVisualization Issue Identified



The scatter plot above demonstrates a critical labeling problem: with 322 data points, the labels overlap significantly, making individual student IDs impossible to read.
The plot does show a positive correlation (r = 0.831) between Mathematics and English scores, but the overlapping labels obscure this insight and make the visualization ineffective for identifying individual students.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
3.2 The Solution: Using ggrepel

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")














TipImprovement Achieved



Using geom_label_repel() instead of geom_label() successfully eliminates label overlap through intelligent automatic positioning. Each of the 322 student IDs is now readable and traceable to its corresponding data point, while maintaining the visualization of the strong positive correlation (r = 0.831) between subjects.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
3.3 Extended Analysis: Selective Labeling Strategy

# Calculate performance metrics
exam_data_labeled <- exam_data %>%
  mutate(
    total_score = MATHS + ENGLISH + SCIENCE,
    performance_label = case_when(
      total_score >= quantile(total_score, 0.9) ~ ID,
      total_score <= quantile(total_score, 0.1) ~ ID,
      TRUE ~ ""
    )
  )

# Identify thresholds
top_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.9)
bottom_10_threshold <- quantile(exam_data_labeled$total_score, 0.1)
n_labeled <- sum(exam_data_labeled$performance_label != "")

ggplot(data=exam_data_labeled, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = total_score), size = 3, alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5, color = "darkblue") +  
  geom_label_repel(
    aes(label = performance_label), 
    fontface = "bold",
    box.padding = 0.5,
    point.padding = 0.3,
    segment.color = 'grey50',
    max.overlaps = 20
  ) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100), ylim=c(0,100)) +
  labs(
    title = "English vs Maths: Top/Bottom 10% Performers Labeled",
    subtitle = "Color gradient shows total score across all subjects",
    x = "Mathematics Score",
    y = "English Score",
    color = "Total Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "right")














NoteStrategic Labeling Analysis



Selective Approach: Instead of labeling all 322 students, this visualization strategically labels only 68 students (21.1% of the sample) - specifically the top and bottom 10% performers.
Performance Thresholds: - Top 10%: Total score ≥ 254 points - Bottom 10%: Total score ≤ 118.2 points
Key Insight: The color gradient reveals that high performers in Mathematics tend to also score high in English (note the concentration of yellow/bright points in the upper-right quadrant), confirming the strong correlation (r = 0.831) and suggesting that academic ability is relatively consistent across subjects for exceptional students.

text:
6.11 Statistical Hypothesis Testing

6.11.1 Gender Differences Analysis

# T-tests for all subjects
t_maths <- t.test(MATHS ~ GENDER, data = exam_data)
t_english <- t.test(ENGLISH ~ GENDER, data = exam_data)
t_science <- t.test(SCIENCE ~ GENDER, data = exam_data)

# Create summary table
t_test_results <- tibble(
  Subject = c("Mathematics", "English", "Science"),
  Male_Mean = c(
    t_maths$estimate[1],
    t_english$estimate[1],
    t_science$estimate[1]
  ),
  Female_Mean = c(
    t_maths$estimate[2],
    t_english$estimate[2],
    t_science$estimate[2]
  ),
  Difference = Male_Mean - Female_Mean,
  t_statistic = c(t_maths$statistic, t_english$statistic, t_science$statistic),
  p_value = c(t_maths$p.value, t_english$p.value, t_science$p.value),
  Significant = p_value < 0.05
)

                        labels = c("0-10%", "11-20%", "21-30%", "31-50%", "50%+"),
                        include.lowest = TRUE)

# Compare average discount
discount_comparison <- superstore %>%
  mutate(Profit_Status = ifelse(Profit > 0, "Profitable", "Unprofitable")) %>%
  group_by(Profit_Status) %>%
  summarise(
    Avg_Discount = mean(Discount),
    Count = n(),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit)

p4 <- ggplot(regional_performance, aes(x = reorder(Region, Avg_Profit), y = Avg_Profit, fill = Region)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(Avg_Profit, accuracy = 0.1)), 
            hjust = -0.1, size = 4) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format(), limits = c(0, 40)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set3") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Profit per Transaction by Region",
    x = "Region",
    y = "Average Profit"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

p3 + p4

title: In-class Exercise
text:
3.5 Temporal Analysis

# Prepare data for time series analysis
monthly_trends <- superstore %>%
  mutate(
    Year = year(`Order Date`),
    Month = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month")
  ) %>%
  group_by(Month, Category) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Calculate overall monthly metrics
monthly_overall <- superstore %>%
  mutate(Month = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month")) %>%
  group_by(Month) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Profit_Margin = (Total_Profit / Total_Sales) * 100
  )


3.5.1 Visualization: Sales and Profit Trends

p5 <- ggplot(monthly_trends, aes(x = Month, y = Total_Sales, color = Category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Sales Trend by Category",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Total Sales",
    color = "Category"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p6 <- ggplot(monthly_overall, aes(x = Month, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2c3e50", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Profit Margin Trend",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Profit Margin (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p5 / p6

4.1 Summary of Critical Findings

# Create summary table of key metrics
key_metrics <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total Transactions",
    "Total Sales",
    "Total Profit",
    "Overall Profit Margin",
    "Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Loss from Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Most Profitable Category",
    "Least Profitable Sub-Category"
  ),
  Value = c(
    format(nrow(superstore), big.mark = ","),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Sales)),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit)),
    paste0(round((sum(superstore$Profit) / sum(superstore$Sales)) * 100, 2), "%"),
    paste0(format(sum(superstore$Profit < 0), big.mark = ","), 
           " (", round(mean(superstore$Profit < 0) * 100, 1), "%)"),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit[superstore$Profit < 0])),
    "Technology",
    "Tables"
  )
)
